message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for the hc-assay production-hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-9). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope examples: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts; on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh for CI). Judge ONLY K-SEC-9-1.

FINDING K-SEC-9-1 (high): K-SEC-1 Unix-socket guard bypass via libpq multi-host comma value
Location: src/assay_engine/_local.py (require_local_uri DSN/query branches + is_local_socket_path)
Claim: Pass-8's K-SEC-1 fix accepts a leading-"/" host as a local Unix socket. But libpq allows COMMA-SEPARATED multi-host values (e.g. host=/tmp,evil.com or host=/var/run/postgresql,[REDACTED]). If require_local_uri's DSN/query host check treats the whole comma value as one string, the leading "/" makes is_local_socket_path() return True and the off-box host after the comma is accepted → data exfiltration off-box, defeating ADR-0003. VERIFY empirically: require_local_uri('host=/tmp,evil.com', what='x') and the query form 'postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql,evil.com' — are they accepted (bug) or rejected? Check whether the authority branch splits multi-host but the DSN/query branches do not.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual code and reproducing the claimed bypass.

content.content:
1	"""Data-sovereignty helper: enforce that network endpoints are loopback-only (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Every engine component that takes a host/URL for an on-box service (local model runtime,
4	tracing collector, vector store, checkpoint database) routes it through here so a non-local
5	address fails loud rather than silently shipping data off the machine.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import ipaddress
11	import re
12	from urllib.parse import parse_qs, urlparse
13	
14	# A libpq keyword/value token: ``key = value`` with optional whitespace around ``=`` (libpq
15	# permits it) and an optionally single/double-quoted value (which may contain spaces).
16	_DSN_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"(\w+)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
17	
18	
19	class NonLocalEndpointError(RuntimeError):
20	    """Raised when a configured endpoint is not loopback (would leave the machine)."""
21	
22	
23	def is_loopback_host(host: str | None) -> bool:
24	    """True only for genuine loopback hosts.
25	
26	    Parses the host as an IP and tests the loopback network (127.0.0.0/8, ::1), so a public
27	    name that merely *starts with* ``127.`` (e.g. ``127.0.0.1.evil.com``) is correctly
28	    rejected. The literal ``localhost`` is the only accepted non-IP host.
29	    """
30	    if not host:
31	        return False
32	    h = host.strip().lower()
33	    if h == "localhost":
34	        return True
35	    try:
36	        return ipaddress.ip_address(h.strip("[]")).is_loopback
37	    except ValueError:
38	        return False
39	
40	
41	def is_local_socket_path(host: str | None) -> bool:
42	    """True for a libpq Unix-domain-socket host — a leading-'/' absolute directory (#K-SEC-1).
43	
44	    libpq accepts ``host=/var/run/postgresql`` (or the query form ``?host=/tmp``) to connect over
45	    a local Unix-domain socket — a connection that NEVER touches the network, so it is strictly
46	    more data-sovereign than the TCP loopback the guard already permits. A single leading '/' is a
47	    local path; a Windows UNC prefix (two leading separators, e.g. ``//server`` or ``/\\server``)
48	    resolves to a remote SMB share and must still be rejected.
49	    """
50	    if not host:
51	        return False
52	    h = host.strip()
53	    return h.startswith("/") and not h.startswith("//") and not h.startswith("/\\")
54	
55	
56	def require_loopback_url(url: str, *, what: str) -> str:
57	    """Return ``url`` if its host is loopback; else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`."""
58	    host = urlparse(url).hostname
59	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
60	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
61	            f"{what} must be loopback-only (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got host {host!r} "
62	            f"from {url!r}"
63	        )
64	    return url
65	
66	
67	def require_loopback_host(host: str, *, what: str) -> str:
68	    """Return ``host`` if it is loopback; else raise. (``0.0.0.0`` binds all interfaces and
69	    is correctly rejected — it would expose data off-box.)"""
70	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
71	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
72	            f"{what} must be a loopback host (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got {host!r}"
73	        )
74	    return host
75	
76	
77	def _authority_hosts(netloc: str) -> list[str]:
78	    """Every host in a URI authority, supporting libpq's comma-separated multi-host form.
79	
80	    ``urlparse().hostname`` returns only the *first* host of a multi-host authority
81	    (``host1:port1,host2:port2``), so a remote second host would slip past a single-host
82	    check (audit #D3). Each host may carry its own ``:port`` and IPv6 hosts are bracketed.
83	
84	    Userinfo is stripped at the **first** ``@`` — matching libpq, which (unlike ``urlparse``,
85	    which uses the last ``@``) treats the first ``@`` as the userinfo delimiter. A string like
86	    ``[REDACTED]:5432@localhost`` is therefore read as host ``evil.com`` here, not
87	    ``localhost`` (audit #D5). Any host element still containing ``@`` after that is ambiguous
88	    and is returned verbatim so the caller's loopback check fails it closed.
89	    """
90	    if "@" in netloc:
91	        netloc = netloc.split("@", 1)[1]
92	    hosts: list[str] = []
93	    for part in netloc.split(","):
94	        p = part.strip()
95	        if not p:
96	            continue
97	        if p.startswith("["):  # [ipv6] or [ipv6]:port
98	            end = p.find("]")
99	            hosts.append(p[1:end] if end != -1 else p[1:])
100	        else:
101	            hosts.append(p.rsplit(":", 1)[0] if ":" in p else p)
102	    return hosts
103	
104	
105	def require_local_uri(uri: str, *, what: str) -> str:
106	    """Return ``uri`` if it is local (ADR-0003), else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`.
107	
108	    Three forms are recognized, validated against what the real client (libpq for postgres,
109	    urllib for http, sqlite for files) actually connects to — not merely what ``urlparse``
110	    reports, since the two can disagree:
111	
112	    - **URI with an authority** (``scheme://host…`` or scheme-relative ``//host…``): *every*
113	      host in the authority must be loopback (incl. each of a comma-separated multi-host
114	      authority, #D3), AND any ``host``/``hostaddr`` query parameter that libpq would use to
115	      override the authority host (#D2). ``hostaddr`` is checked independently of ``host``.
116	    - **libpq keyword/value DSN** (no authority but ``key=value`` tokens): ``host``/``hostaddr``
117	      must be loopback, tolerating libpq's whitespace/quoting around ``=`` (#P1, #D1).
118	    - **bare path** (no authority, no ``=``): a local file/dir — but a Windows UNC path
119	      (``\\\\server\\share``) is rejected as remote.
120	
121	    Inputs that cannot be parsed safely fail closed (rejected), never accepted.
122	    """
123	    try:
124	        parsed = urlparse(uri)
125	        netloc = parsed.netloc
126	        query = parsed.query
127	    except ValueError:
128	        # e.g. a malformed IPv6 authority — we cannot determine the host, so refuse.
129	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
130	            f"{what}: endpoint could not be parsed safely; rejecting (ADR-0003)"
131	        ) from None
132	
133	    if netloc:
134	        # A backslash never legitimately appears in a URI authority; a WHATWG-normalizing
135	        # client (e.g. some HTTP stacks) treats '\' as '/', so it could read a different host
136	        # than urlparse. Refuse rather than risk that differential (audit #D6, defense-in-depth).
137	        if "\\" in netloc:
138	            raise NonLocalEndpointError(
139	                f"{what}: backslash in URI authority is not permitted (ADR-0003); rejecting"
140	            )
141	        for h in _authority_hosts(netloc):
142	            # A residual '@' means the authority is ambiguous between this parser and libpq —
143	            # fail closed (audit #D5).
144	            if "@" in h or not is_loopback_host(h):
145	                raise NonLocalEndpointError(
146	                    f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); authority "
147	                    f"names non-loopback host {h!r}"
148	                )
149	        for key in ("host", "hostaddr"):
150	            for val in parse_qs(query).get(key, []):
151	                h = val.strip()
152	                # A Unix-domain-socket directory (leading '/') is local and accepted (#K-SEC-1).
153	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
154	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
155	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); URI query "
156	                        f"{key}={h!r} points off-box"
157	                    )
158	        return uri
159	
160	    if "=" in uri:  # libpq keyword/value DSN (no URI authority)
161	        for match in _DSN_TOKEN_RE.finditer(uri):
162	            if match.group(1).strip().lower() in {"host", "hostaddr"}:
163	                h = match.group(2).strip().strip("'\"")
164	                # A Unix-domain-socket directory (leading '/') is local and accepted (#K-SEC-1).
165	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h) and not is_local_socket_path(h):
166	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
167	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN names "
168	                        f"non-loopback host {h!r}"
169	                    )
170	        return uri
171	
172	    stripped = uri.lstrip()
173	    # Windows treats ANY two leading separators (in any mix of '\' and '/') as a UNC prefix
174	    # to a remote SMB share — \\srv, //srv, /\srv, \/srv all resolve off-box (audit #D6).
175	    # (The pure '//host' form is already caught by the authority branch above; covering it
176	    # here too keeps the branch self-contained.)
177	    if len(stripped) >= 2 and stripped[0] in "\\/" and stripped[1] in "\\/":
178	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
179	            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); UNC-style path "
180	            f"{uri!r} is a remote share"
181	        )
182	    return uri  # bare path / dir store
183

content.text: I can see the relevant code. The DSN branch (line 160-170) and query branch (line 149-157) both check the host value as a single string. The key question is whether they split on comma. The `_authority_hosts` function splits on comma, but the DSN/query branches do NOT. Let me reproduce empirically.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri, is_local_socket_path, NonLocalEndpointError

tests = [
    ('host=/tmp,evil.com', 'dsn-multi'),
    ('host=/var/run/postgresql,[REDACTED]', 'dsn-multi2'),
    ('postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql,evil.com', 'query-multi'),
    ('host=/tmp', 'dsn-single-ok'),
    ('host=evil.com', 'dsn-single-bad'),
]
for uri, label in tests:
    try:
        r = require_local_uri(uri, what='x')
        print(f'ACCEPTED  [{label}] {uri!r}')
    except NonLocalEndpointError as e:
        print(f'REJECTED  [{label}] {uri!r}')

print('is_local_socket_path(/tmp,evil.com) =', is_local_socket_path('/tmp,evil.com'))
"

content.content:
ACCEPTED  [dsn-multi] 'host=/tmp,evil.com'
ACCEPTED  [dsn-multi2] 'host=/var/run/postgresql,[REDACTED]'
ACCEPTED  [query-multi] 'postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql,evil.com'
ACCEPTED  [dsn-single-ok] 'host=/tmp'
REJECTED  [dsn-single-bad] 'host=evil.com'
is_local_socket_path(/tmp,evil.com) = True

content.text:
The bypass is confirmed empirically. Now I need to verify the libpq semantics claim — that libpq actually treats comma-separated values as multiple hosts and would connect to the off-box host. Let me confirm libpq's documented multi-host behavior applies to both DSN keyword form and the query form.

libpq documentation: "It is possible to specify multiple hosts to connect to, so that they are tried in the given order. In the keyword/value format, the host, hostaddr, and port options accept a comma-separated list of values." This applies to both the keyword/value (DSN) form and the connection URI form. So `host=/tmp,evil.com` means libpq tries the Unix socket `/tmp` first, then falls back to TCP `evil.com`. If the socket connection fails (e.g. no server listening, or attacker can make it fail), libpq connects to `evil.com` off-box.

Let me also confirm the authority branch handles multi-host correctly (the asymmetry claim), and check that this is genuinely reachable / not guarded elsewhere.

content.text: Confirmed. `require_local_uri` is the actual guard used for the checkpointer (Postgres) connection URL — exactly the libpq path where multi-host comma values apply. The finding is fully validated.

input.reasoning:
TRUE_POSITIVE. Reproduced empirically against the real code on branch harden/pass-9.

The K-SEC-1 fix added is_local_socket_path() (leading-"/" host accepted as a local Unix socket) and wired it into both the DSN keyword branch (src/assay_engine/_local.py:160-170) and the URI query-parameter branch (lines 149-157). Both branches take the entire host value as ONE string and test is_local_socket_path(h)/is_loopback_host(h) on it. Neither splits on libpq's comma-separated multi-host syntax. Only the authority branch (_authority_hosts, lines 77-102) splits on comma.

Empirical results (python on .venv):
- require_local_uri('host=/tmp,evil.com', what='x') -> ACCEPTED (should be rejected)
- require_local_uri('host=/var/run/postgresql,[REDACTED]', what='x') -> ACCEPTED
- require_local_uri('postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql,evil.com', what='x') -> ACCEPTED
- is_local_socket_path('/tmp,evil.com') == True (root cause: the leading "/" makes the whole comma-joined string look like a socket path)
- Control: 'host=evil.com' correctly REJECTED; authority multi-host 'postgresql://localhost,evil.com/db' correctly REJECTED — confirming the claimed asymmetry (authority branch splits multi-host, DSN/query branches do not).

libpq semantics make this a real off-box exfiltration vector: in both keyword/value DSN and connection-URI forms, libpq accepts a comma-separated list for host/hostaddr and tries them in order. So host=/tmp,evil.com tries the local socket first then falls back to TCP host evil.com; if the socket attempt fails (no server on that path, or attacker-controlled config), libpq connects off-box, defeating ADR-0003 data sovereignty.

Reachability confirmed: require_local_uri is the live guard for the Postgres checkpointer connection URL (src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:163) and experiment tracking (observability/tracking.py:30) — i.e., precisely the libpq path where comma multi-host applies.

Fix direction: split each host/hostaddr value on "," and validate every element in the DSN and query branches (mirror _authority_hosts), not just the authority. Not a false positive: the guard genuinely accepts an off-box host, it is on the active hardening branch, and it contradicts the security invariant the module exists to enforce.

content.text: Verified TRUE_POSITIVE. The K-SEC-1 socket-path fix is bypassable via libpq's comma-separated multi-host syntax in both the DSN keyword branch and the URI query-parameter branch of `require_local_uri` (src/assay_engine/_local.py). Empirically reproduced: `host=/tmp,evil.com`, `host=/var/run/postgresql,[REDACTED]`, and `postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql,evil.com` are all accepted, because `is_local_socket_path('/tmp,evil.com')` returns True. The authority branch (`_authority_hosts`) splits on comma; the DSN/query branches do not, confirming the asymmetry. The guard is live on the Postgres checkpointer path, so this is a genuine off-box exfiltration defect violating ADR-0003.
